items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’m plagued by vague misgivings again...
Shpak lost a tape recorder,
the ambassador lost a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Core phrase «терзают смутные сомнения» is well rendered as elevated/performative (“plagued by vague misgivings”), preserving the mock-heroic tone. However, the second clause is mistranslated in meaning: «у Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон» in context is a suspicious parallel about missing items (each had/lost one), not possession. The provided translation correctly switches to “lost,” matching the reference analysis. This preserves the joke (connecting two thefts across eras) and avoids the incorrect “has” reading. No issues.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—very pleased.
Tsar—very pleased, Tsar.
Very pleased—hello, Tsar.
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation preserves the key comic mechanism: repetitive, banal politeness formulas (“very pleased”) clashing with the absurdity of introducing oneself as “Tsar,” and the drunken/awkward loop. It is not identical to the reference (it omits the explicit “I’m the Tsar” once), but the effect remains: he keeps saying “Tsar” as if it were a name/title in a modern greeting ritual. Minor loss of clarity that he is introducing himself (vs. addressing), but still readable in context.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical in meaning and function; preserves the sudden authoritarian persona and comic imperiousness. No issues.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to reference; preserves archaic/high-register melancholy and the comic mismatch with the modern setting. No issues.

items.translation:
They walled me up...
They walled me up, the demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Very close to reference and keeps the key feature: “demons” as the subject and the paranoid-mystical framing of a mundane technical situation. “Walled me up” is slightly more like being bricked into a wall than “walled me in,” but still communicates entrapment and preserves the joke. Minor nuance issue only.

items.translation: See what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Captures the religious exclamation and causal attribution of a “miracle,” which is the comedic point. Slightly less mock-biblical/ceremonial than “Behold the power…,” but still elevated enough and clear. No material loss.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Meaning is conveyed (a command for all to dance), but the creative property in the reference is the quasi-announcer/MC phrasing that sounds like a formal invitation/open-floor announcement, heightening the anachronistic party vibe. “Everybody dance!” is more generic and less characterful; it reduces the comic ‘event-host’ register. Comprehension is fine, humor somewhat flattened.

items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Delivers the pragmatic function (self-satisfied reaction to a fortuitous find) and keeps it punchy. Slightly shifts from “I dropped in at the right time” to a more general “lucky break,” but the comic beat remains. No significant issue.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
and yes! Overseas delicacy...
Eggplant spread!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Successfully avoids calling eggplant spread “caviar,” matching the reference’s key warning and preserving the Soviet-satire inversion. Rhythm and escalation (“and yes! overseas…”) are kept. No issues.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
all gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,
three domestic cigarette cases,
a suede jacket... three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Preserves the comedic inventory cadence and the trailing, self-correcting count (“…three.”). “Domestic cigarette cases” correctly keeps «отечественных» as a prestige marker. No issues.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!
And they’ll cure you too!
And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Matches the reference closely and keeps the calm, rhythmic resignation that turns the psychiatric threat into a shared absurdist punchline. No issues.

summary.interpretation: 0.82 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.16 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
